Name: jacobian_latency_optimization
Title: Jacobian-Based Latency Optimization for PAC-Bayes Inference Protocols
Short Hypothesis: Can a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework optimize inference latency in PAC-Bayes frameworks while maintaining accuracy and stability?
Related Work: Existing literature on PAC-Bayes frameworks primarily focuses on theoretical bounds and model complexity, such as those in hybrid models for financial forecasting and real-time anomaly detection. However, there is minimal exploration of Jacobian-based methods for optimizing inference latency specifically in PAC-Bayes contexts. This proposal distinguishes itself by directly targeting latency as a core metric in the PAC-Bayes framework, rather than treating it as a secondary concern.
Abstract: This proposal explores a novel approach to optimizing inference latency in machine learning models through a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework integrated with PAC-Bayes frameworks. The central hypothesis posits that targeted layer-skipping, guided by the Jacobian matrix of model activations, can significantly reduce inference time while preserving accuracy and stability. By framing the optimization within the PAC-Bayes framework, we can derive strict, enforceable latency budgets that allow for real-time deployment in latency-critical applications. We will investigate the trade-offs between accuracy and latency in various model architectures, applying our methods to benchmark datasets across different domains. The proposed framework aims to establish a clear Pareto frontier for throughput and accuracy, ultimately contributing to the development of more efficient and practical machine learning systems.
Experiments: 1. Develop a baseline PAC-Bayes model and measure its performance in terms of accuracy and latency.
2. Implement the Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism, systematically varying the number of skipped layers based on Jacobian norms.
3. Conduct experiments on standard datasets (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR-10) and measure the throughput-accuracy trade-off using metrics such as F1-score and inference time.
4. Evaluate the stability of the model outputs relative to perturbations in input data to ensure the integrity of the optimization mechanism.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The proposed layer-skipping mechanism may lead to degraded model performance if not carefully calibrated.
2. There may be a lack of generalizability across different architectures, necessitating further experimentation.
3. The complexity of implementing the Jacobian-based optimization may introduce additional overhead in certain contexts.
